text:
Problem Overview

This problem introduces students to a percent. A percent is a part-to-whole comparison where the whole is 100. Students experience visual ways to represent a percent using both grids and tape diagrams. The grids begin as 100 grids, but then students are challenged by a grid that is not broken into 100 pieces. Students will look at percentages that are more than and less than 100%.

Implementation Note: The Initial Challenge will provide a chance for you to formatively assess if your students are making sense of representing a percent. If students can represent the 78% on both the grid and the bar, they can move on to the What If...? situations. If the whole class has questions about how to use the grid and bar, you may want to have a quick conversation about the two diagrams. Then, move the students on to the rest of the What If...? situations.

Arc of Learning: Exploration

Standards: See your state alignment chart.

NWDYK

What is a percent? How is percent like a ratio?

Emerging Mathematical Ideas

  • Begin making sense of percents by connection to earlier part-to-whole ratio comparison statements, with the distinction that this time the whole is 100.
  • Notice that
    • percents are part-to-whole ratios that always compare to a total of 100;
    • percents tell you how many out of 100;
    • 10 × 10 grids and bar grids are useful representations for percents; and
    • percent ratios make comparing ratios quick and easy.

Launch (Getting Started)

Connecting to Prior Knowledge

Show students the grid and the bar, which is a version of a tape diagram. (Portryal) Learning Aid 4.1A: 100 Grid and 100 Bar has a picture of both, but you will want to hide the title.

Suggested Questions

  • What do you notice, and what do you wonder? (Agency, Identity, Ownership)

Answers will vary. Grid: Students may see that the grid is 10 rows by 10 columns. Students may see 1 whole grid broken into 100 pieces. Students may see 100 pieces. Bar (tape diagram): Students may see that the bar is broken into 10 pieces and each of those is broken into 10 pieces. Students may count the pieces in the first section, then count by 10 for each section. Students may count the 100 individual pieces.

Introduce the grid and the bar as ways to represent amounts out of 100. Students will use the diagrams in the problem.

Ask students about their experiences with percent:

  • Have you heard "percent" being used to describe something? When was it used? What did it mean?
  • What do you know about percentages?
  • Why do you think percent is used?

• Answers will vary. Students may know percentages from grades or sport contexts.
  • Answers wil vary.
  • Answers will vary.
